steps.explanation: List the known angles. Triangle $DEF$: $\angle D = 40^\circ$, $\angle E = 95^\circ$. Triangle $RST$: $\angle R = 40^\circ$, $\angle S = 95^\circ$. We already have two pairs of equal corresponding angles: $\angle D = \angle R$ and $\angle E = \angle S$.

The AA (Angle-Angle) Similarity Theorem states: if two angles of one triangle equal two angles of another triangle, the triangles are similar. Two pairs of equal angles are sufficient — no third angle is needed.

Confirm the third angles are implicitly equal anyway. Angle sum: $\angle F = 180^\circ - 40^\circ - 95^\circ = 45^\circ$ and $\angle T = 180^\circ - 40^\circ - 95^\circ = 45^\circ$. They are forced equal by the first two pairs.

Evaluate all choices:
  • A: The measure of angle $F$ — We already have two equal angle pairs, so AA similarity is already established without knowing $\angle F$. Incorrect.
  • B: The measure of angle $T$ — Same reasoning; $\angle T$ is not needed. Incorrect.
  • C: Both angle $F$ and angle $T$ — Neither is needed; AA criterion is already satisfied. Incorrect.
  • D: No additional information is needed — Two pairs of equal angles ($40^\circ = 40^\circ$ and $95^\circ = 95^\circ$) are already given, which satisfies AA similarity. That matches choice D — D is correct.
